### Unabyss 1.1

Hey everyone

Since the launch, we went heads-down and shipped a batch of improvements. Quick rundown:

Imports improved - both in terms of reliability and efficiency. The big one. If you tried Unabyss early and a source synced halfway or dropped data, that's fixed. Every import runs clean. And we re able to build even more detailed context for you.

Better cross-agent memory. We upgraded the store tool in our MCP - so if you run more than one agent, they finally share context. What you tell Claude, ChatGPT picks up too. No more re-explaining yourself to every assistant. [...] Unabyss

Hey PH 👋 Philip here, co-founder of Unabyss.

What is Unabyss? Unabyss is your personal context layer - a single, structured vault of your identity, knowledge, and preferences that any AI app or agent can access instantly, with you in full control of what gets shared and with whom.

The Problem Every AI tool you use starts from zero. You re-explain your role, your goals, your tone, your company - over and over. And when you finally do build up context inside one platform, it's trapped there. ChatGPT memory doesn't follow you to Claude. Claude Projects don't talk to Cursor. The more AI tools you adopt, the worse it gets. [...] ### Unabyss

Hey PH Philip here, co-founder of Unabyss.

What is Unabyss? Unabyss is your personal context layer - a single, structured vault of your identity, knowledge, and preferences that any AI app or agent can access instantly, with you in full control of what gets shared and with whom.

The Problem Every AI tool you use starts from zero. You re-explain your role, your goals, your tone, your company - over and over. And when you finally do build up context inside one platform, it's trapped there. ChatGPT memory doesn't follow you to Claude. Claude Projects don't talk to Cursor. The more AI tools you adopt, the worse it gets.

results.title: Issue: Claude Code is unusable for complex engineering ...
results.content: I appreciate the work done here. Been having this feeling that things have got worse recently but didn't think it could be model related.  The most frustrating aspect recently (I have learned and accepted that Claude produces bad code and probably always did, mea culpa) is the non-compliance. Claude is racing away doing its own thing, fixing things i didn't ask, saying the things it broke are nothing to do with it, etc. Quite unpleasant to work with.  The stuff about token consumption is also interesting. Minimax/Composer have this habit of extensive thinking and it is said to be their strength but it seems like that comes at a price of huge output token consumption. If you compare non-thinking models, there is a gap there but, imo, given that the eventual code quality within huge [...] was spent in conversations which were eventually ranked as frustrating.  Median time to frustration was 25 messages, and on average, each message from Claude has about a baseline 5% chance of being frustrating. Frustration by chat length actually matches this 5% baseline of IID Bernoullis -- which is surprising and interesting, as this should not be IID at all.  Frustration types:  - Wrong answers – 14% of sessions, 31% of frustration  - Instruction Following – 11% of sessions, 25% of frustration  - Overcomplication – 8% of sessions, 18% of frustration  - Destructive Actions (e.g. requesting to delete something or commit a change to prod) – 3% of sessions, 8% of frustration  - Non-responsive (service outages leading to non-response) 2% of sessions  - Miscommunication 2% of sessions  - [...] the problem correctly.  Late frustrations led to the highest levels of frustration, 29% of the time.  I'm a data scientist — my most frustrating work with Claude was data cleaning/repair (a complex backfill) issues -- with 75% of sessions marked frustrating due to overcomplicating, instruction following, or destructive actions).  The best (least frustrating) workflows for DS were code-review, scoped feature work (with tickets), data validation, and config/setup tasks and automation.  Ad-hoc query work ended up in between -- ad-hoc requests were generally bootstrapping queries or doing rough analysis on good data.  Side note: all of my interactions with the /buddy feature were flagged as high frustration ("furious"). That was a false positive over mock arguing with it, but did provide a

results.title: Ask HN: Why is my Claude experience so bad? What am I doing wrong? | Hacker News
results.content: see anything.  2nd pass, syntax error.  3rd try I ask it to redo everything from scratch. It now has a working slider, but the landscape/portrait is still broken.  4th try, it manages to fix the landscape/portrait issue, but now the issue is that the controls are behind the display so I have to reload the page.  5th try, it manages to fix this issue, but now it is squished again.  6th try, I ask it to try again from scratch. This time it gives me a syntax error.  This is so frustrating. |     |  |  |  --- | | |  |  | kpil 4 months ago  | next (javascript:void(0))   The truth is that there is a lot of hype. You need to be reasonably experienced and guide it.  First, you need to know that Claude will create nonsensical code. On a macro level it's not exactly smart it just has a lot of [...] months ago  | parent | next (javascript:void(0))   I think this reply might have been downvoted for being a bit glib, but the superpowers plugin took my Claude Code experience from mostly frustrating to nearly-magical I’m not a software engineer by training nor trade, so caveats apply, but I found that the brainstorming -> plan writing -> plan execution flow provided by the skills in this plugin helps immensely with extracting assumptions and unsaid preferences into a comprehensive plan—-very similar to the guidance elsewhere in this thread, except automated/guided along by the plugin skills | | | |  |  | LeoPanthera 4 months ago  | prev | next (javascript:void(0))   Are you using plan mode? | | | |  |  | d--b 4 months ago  | prev | next (javascript:void(0))   what language/framework are [...] reframe the prompt and the problems at hand and try again.  I'm not totally sure about the language you are using, but syntax errors typically happens if it "forgets" to update some of the code, and very seldom just in a single file or edit.  I like to create a design.md and think a bit on my own, or maybe prompt to create it with a high level problem to get going, and make sure it's in the context (and mentioned in the prompts) | | | |  |  | andrei\_says\_ 4 months ago  | parent | next (javascript:void(0))   Am I crazy thinking that interacting with such a system is a nightmarishly frustrating way to write code? Like trying to write with a wet noodle - always off in some way.  Write the code feels way more precise and not less efficient. | | | |  |  | sh4rks 4 months ago  | root | parent

The difference

## Don't answer questions - just work.

Without Unabyss

With Unabyss

### What you tell one AI, they all know.

No copy-pasting between tools that don't talk.

Start now

How it works

## Connect once. Every tool works with and builds the same memory. [...] ChatGPT Memory and Claude Memory each remember conversations inside that one tool - the memory doesn't travel with you. Unabyss sits above all your tools: it pulls context from where your work actually lives and serves it via MCP. Switch from Claude to Cursor to ChatGPT and the context comes with you, not just what you told one chatbot.

A context file works - until it goes stale. It's a snapshot: your role, your project, your priorities change, and the file doesn't. You're stuck manually updating and re-pasting it into every tool. Unabyss stays connected to your actual sources, so it updates itself - and it also pulls from what you do inside Claude, ChatGPT, or Cursor, feeding that back in. A .md file can't do that.

results.title: I stopped treating my AI like a toy and gave it a brain with ...
results.content:
What I personally loved while testing Unabyss:

   It is user‑owned, not locked to one vendor.
   It is pre‑extracted from all the places you already live (email, docs, calendar, repos), instead of something you’re constantly rewriting by hand.
   It is cross‑platform, available via MCP to any compatible client, from Claude desktop to Cursor to future agents you haven’t even tried yet.

One connection, every tool. You can stop copy‑pasting yourself for the rest of your career. hahahhaha!

So instead of wiring agents straight into Gmail, Slack, or GitHub, I connected _those_ to Unabyss.

The agent then reads from my context vault. Basically if you are doing this [...] None of these memories talk to each other.

You (or I):

   Re‑explain your role and company.
   Paste the same “brand voice” prompt everywhere.
   Re‑link the same Notion pages and Google Docs.
   Re‑define your ICP, positioning, and constraints.

And when something changes -you hire, pivot, launch, rebrand — you have to go update that story in ten different places. ( This was exactly my life, before Unabyss)

The more AI tools you adopt, the worse the context problem gets.

Unabyssis a personal context vault for your AI tools.

Instead of each app trying to build its own scattered memory of you, Unabyss creates one structured, self‑updating “you” that every MCP‑compatible tool can tap into (Claude, Claude Code, Cursor, OpenClaw, and more).

Here is how it works, step by step: [...] Image 6

Get started in seconds

As those underlying tools change (new docs, new emails, new events), Unabyss refreshes your context automatically instead of you touching 20 different prompt files.

4. Any compatible AI app can now read that vault via MCP

   When Claude, Cursor, or an agent needs context, they don’t guess.
   They _pull exactly the files you’ve allowed them to see_, at the depth you choose.

So instead of “memory as a side effect” (whatever a single chatbot picked up in a few chats), you get context as infrastructure: deliberate, structured, re‑usable.

### Memory vs context: why this needed to exist

Unabyss has a line I really like:

> _“Memory is a side effect. Context is infrastructure.”_

Do you understand how powerful this is? It means that
